nodes.text: In the kitchenette at the back of the bookstore the cat — Petra's cat, who was old enough that her name had stopped being used — was on her wool pillow on the chair beside the radiator. The cat opened one eye when the four of them came in. The cat closed the eye.

Petra had put the kettle on. Petra had set out four mugs. Petra had not, in her movements at the counter, asked anyone anything. She was working in the way she worked when she had decided to give a person the space the person needed by giving them the rhythm of coffee being made.
Lena sat at the table by the kitchenette window. Cale sat across from her. Kai stood by the door. Petra brought the coffee. She set the cups on the table. She sat down at the end.
She did not, for a count, say anything.

I have been in this town for forty-one years. I bought this building in '11. I have lived above the bookstore since '15. The mural on the wall outside my back door has been there since you painted it in '50. The face on the mural this morning is — Lena. The face is new.
The face is — by my reading at the dumpster — the kind of face I have been seeing in this town in the last three years on people I do not, by my own ability to place them, recognize.

The face I have seen at the corner of Hemlock and Birch at six in the morning twice this summer. The face I saw at the Daily Grind in the corner table on a morning in May when I came in for the Tuesday meeting. The face I saw on the back of a Subaru leaving the foundation's lot at Ona Beach in June.

The face is — Lena, the face has been at the edges of this town for some time. The face has not been a person I have been able to put a name to. The face has been the face of a young woman I have been seeing without registering until I am back at my apartment three hours later and have realized I have seen her. The face on the wall this morning — Lena. The face on the wall this morning is that face.
Yeah. The face — that's the daughter Nate Dean told me about in February of '94 in the basement on the Alsea River. That's who we've been at, for two years here at this table, with Brandon's papers.

That's the name I said out loud for the first time on Monday morning at Tem's cabin in front of Kai and Finn. I had a name. I didn't have a face. This morning I have the face. I — I am sitting with it.
Petra did not, for a count, do anything.
She's been at the edges of this town. She's been here. I haven't, in the years I've been seeing her, been able to register that I was seeing her. I am registering this morning.
She has been here for some years. And what's on the wall is — by your work, Lena — what she's now been given.

She didn't come at six. I would have seen. The bookstore opens onto the front and the alley both. She wasn't at the front when I went out at six twenty-three for the morning paper. She wasn't at the back. If she had been at either I would have known.
The corner at the far end of the alley.
I have been seeing her walk into things in this town for three years. The first time was when I saw her walk into the door of the cheese-factory-town's old creamery, which had been closed since '49.

I had been driving back from a property thing in old-Waldport. I had pulled over at the creamery to take a phone call. I had seen her — by what I have been figuring out in the years since — I had seen her cross the lot at the creamery and walk into the door. The door had not opened. She had walked into it.
I had registered at the time that I had seen something I did not have a category for. I had not, until this morning at the back of the alley, had a category. The face on the wall is the category.
How long has she been here.

Three years. By my account. Could be longer. I am sixty-one. My eyes do not catch the small movements they caught at forty. There could be ten years of her at the edges of my attention and I would have registered three of them. Tem may have more.

They sat at the kitchenette table for an hour. Petra made a second pot of coffee. The cat woke up and walked across the table once, in the way the cat had been walking across the table once a morning since 2049, and went back to her pillow. The rain on the bookstore's back window was steady. The town behind the bookstore was the town.

Lena did not, in the hour, drink much. She held the cup in both hands and looked at the cup. She had been at the wall and she had been at the asphalt and now she was at the kitchenette table and the count, by some accident of how the morning was working, had stopped. The count had been at six hundred and twelve when she had finished the collarbone. The count was at six hundred and twelve still. The count had not gone up since.
She registered this at maybe nine-thirty.
The count was the count of the strokes. The strokes were done. The count is done.

I'm going to feel the absence for some hours. The count has been with me since Wednesday. It's been the leak, Cale — the leak that's been doing the listening to me. The listening is over. The face is on the wall. The substrate is done with me. That's how I read it.
I do not think the substrate is done with you.
Because the painting is one strand. That was your strand. The substrate cleared the wall for it. That strand is done. The others are still running.

The strand at the cabin with the bowls. The strand at Cape Perpetua with the crow. The strand at the bakery on Saturday morning. The strand with Roy. The substrate has finished one piece. It has many pieces.

But I think — I think the count is done for now. I think the next thing the substrate is going to ask of you is going to be a different thing. I do not know what. I do not think it is — I do not think it is more count.
She drank from the coffee for the first time in the hour. The coffee was cool.
The bookstore has a couch in the back office. Take it.
She got up. She looked at Cale and Kai.
I'll be up in two hours. Go up to the cabin if you need to. Come back at eight tonight, Kai.
I'll go up. I'll tell Tem the count has stopped. I'll tell her you painted. I'll tell her you painted what we — what you painted.
Tell her the eyes opened a degree. Tell her the figure was at the corner. Tell her she walked into the brick.

She walked into the back office. The couch was the couch. She lay down. Petra came in behind her with a wool blanket and put it over her without saying anything. Petra went back out and closed the door behind her.
Lena lay on the couch. She closed her eyes. The count was not running.

For the first time in a week the silence behind her closed eyes was the silence of an apartment whose tenant had stepped out for the morning. The count had been in the apartment. The count had moved. The substrate had not gone away. The substrate was still in her — she could feel the listening, low and patient, the way a person feels the breathing of a sleeper in the next room. The listening was the substrate's. The substrate was sleeping for an hour.
